coredata.dc:title: Graphitic carbon nitride supported platinum nanocomposites for rapid and sensitive colorimetric detection of mercury ions
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yi-Wei
Wang, Lixing
An, Fengping
Xu, Hui
Yin, Zejin
Tang, Shurong
Yang, Huang-Hao
Song, Hongbo
coredata.dc:description: In this study, graphitic carbon nitride supported platinum nanocomposites (g-C3N4/PtNPs) have been synthesized for the first time by an ultrasonic-assisted chemical reduction method. By using g-C3N4 as the stabilizer, Pt ions could be reduced to PtNPs by NaBH4 and uniformly deposited on the surface of g-C3N4. The resulting g-C3N4/PtNPs exhibited enhanced catalytic activity for the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) compared to that of g-C3N4 or PtNPs alone. After interaction with Hg2+, the catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs was effectively inhibited due to the formation of HgPt amalgam. On the basis of this effect, a novel label free colorimetric sensor has been developed for sensitive detection of Hg2+ through the g-C3N4/PtNPs mediated catalytic reaction. A detection limit as low as 1.23 nM was achieved. This assay also exhibited excellent selectivity toward Hg2+ over other metal ions. In addition, it was successfully applied to the determination of Hg2+ in real water samples. In view of the advantages, such as simple operation, cost-effective, rapid response and naked-eye observation, the developed colorimetric sensor hold great potential for the detection of toxic Hg2+ in environmental and biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride/platinum nanocomposite
Mercury ions
Colorimetric sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of the colorimetric sensor for Hg2+ detection
Effect of Hg2+ on the catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs
Sensing performance of the colorimetric assay for Hg2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of g-C3N4 nanosheet and g-C3N4/PtNPs nanocomposites.
Comparison the catalytic activity of different materials for the oxidation of TMB without addition of H2O2 (a) g-C3N4/PtNPs (b) PtNPs (c) g-C3N4.
Feasibility investigation of the colorimetric sensor for Hg2+ detection (a) 0 nM (b) 200 nM (c) 400 nM (The insert shows the corresponding photographs of the solution).
Hg 4f electron spectra of g-C3N4/PtNPs hybrid after interaction with Hg2+.

Absorption spectra of TMB solution under different concentrations of Hg2+ (0–500 nM). (Inset: The linear relationship between absorption signal and Hg2+ concentration. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements).
Selectivity investigation of the colorimetric sensor toward different metal ions (Hg2+: 500 nM, Cu2+ and Pb2+: 2 μM, other ions: 5 μM).
Schematic diagram of the g-C3N4/PtNPs based colorimetric sensor for Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Graphitic carbon nitride supported platinum nanocomposites for rapid and sensitive colorimetric detection of mercury ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Predictive Microbiology and Chemical Residual Analysis, College of Food Science, Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University, Fuzhou, 350002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Predictive Microbiology and Chemical Residual Analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Faculty of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350108, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Lab of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety of the MOE, State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Lab of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety of the MOE
State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, graphitic carbon nitride supported platinum nanocomposites (g-C3N4/PtNPs) have been synthesized for the first time by an ultrasonic-assisted chemical reduction method. By using g-C3N4 as the stabilizer, Pt ions could be reduced to PtNPs by NaBH4 and uniformly deposited on the surface of g-C3N4. The resulting g-C3N4/PtNPs exhibited enhanced catalytic activity for the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) compared to that of g-C3N4 or PtNPs alone. After interaction with Hg2+, the catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs was effectively inhibited due to the formation of HgPt amalgam. On the basis of this effect, a novel label free colorimetric sensor has been developed for sensitive detection of Hg2+ through the g-C3N4/PtNPs mediated catalytic reaction. A detection limit as low as 1.23 nM was achieved. This assay also exhibited excellent selectivity toward Hg2+ over other metal ions. In addition, it was successfully applied to the determination of Hg2+ in real water samples. In view of the advantages, such as simple operation, cost-effective, rapid response and naked-eye observation, the developed colorimetric sensor hold great potential for the detection of toxic Hg2+ in environmental and biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions caused environmental pollution has become a serious problem in the world. Due to the accumulative and toxic properties, they can induce health risks to humans even at low concentrations. Mercury ion (Hg2+) is one of the most ubiquitous heavy metal ions. Excessive intake of Hg2+ will lead to perpetual damage to the liver, kidney and central nervous system [1]. Therefore, developing a sensitive and rapid method for the determination of trace amount of Hg2+ in environmental samples is of fundamental significance. Recently, colorimetric sensors have received great attention due to the advantages of easy operation, fast response, signal visibility (instrument-free), applicable for on-site and high throughput analysis.

Compared with natural enzymes, nanoenzymes are more stable, easy preparation, high yield, low cost, structure and catalytic activity tunable. Aroused by these advantages, a series of nanomaterials with peroxidase-mimic activity have been synthesized and applied to the colorimetric detection of various targets [2–6]. Among the nanoenzymes, hybrid nanomaterials, with the combined properties of each component and enhanced catalytic performance are particularly impressive [7,8]. Due to the unique optical, electronic and catalytic properties, noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) have been widely used in the sensing procedure [9,10]. However, most of the synthesis of NPs required stabilizers to avoid aggregation phenomena. These chemical/biochemical stabilizers around the NPs greatly limited their catalytic activity [11]. Recently, carbon materials (such as graphene, carbon nanotube, graphene oxide etc.) supported metal NPs composites with high stability and catalytic ability were explored to solve this problem [12–14].

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a new type of carbon-based material. It can be synthesized in large scale by a simple thermal pyrolysis method through low cost N-rich precursors such as melamine, ammonium thiocyanate, urea and thiourea, etc. [15,16]. Through a “green” liquid exfoliation route from bulk g-C3N4 in water, ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheet with atomic-scale thickness can be effectively prepared [17]. The high stability, excellent biocompatibility, high quantum yields, nontoxicity and semiconductor like band structure of the obtained g-C3N4 nanosheet [18], making it a promising fluorescent probe with wide application in the fields of sensing [19–21], bioimaging [17], drug delivery [22], photoelectrocatalysis [23], oxygen reduction reaction [24] and degradation [25]. Recently, various g-C3N4-based fluorescence sensors have been developed for the detection of metal ions and biological molecules [26–28]. In contrast, very rare attention has been paid to g-C3N4 nanosheet or its nanocomposites for colorimetric sensing based on the peroxidase-like or oxidase-like activity.

With high specific surface area, atomically thick two-dimensional structure, the g-C3N4 nanosheet should be superior to some carbon materials, such as carbon dots or graphene quantum dots, in aspect of integrating with other functional materials. In this work, a novel g-C3N4/PtNPs nanocomposite was successfully synthesized by a facile one-step approach with high stability, superior dispersibility and enhanced oxidase-like activity. Unlike most of the reported peroxidase mimetic enzymes which need additional oxidant in the catalytic reaction, TMB oxidation can be effectively catalyzed by g-C3N4/PtNPs to produce colored product without the addition of H2O2. Interestingly, we found that the catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs can be significantly inhibited by Hg2+, which can be utilized for rapid and selective detection of trace levels of Hg2+ in aqueous solution. The rigid 2-D structure of g-C3N4 enables the exposure of most of the attached PtNPs to the environment, which is benefit to improve the sensitivity.

3,3′,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine hydrochloride (TMB·HCl) was purchased from Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Other reagents, such as hydrochloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6·6H2O), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), mercuric nitrate and acetic acid etc. were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The regents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (Millipore, ≥18.2 MΩ cm) was used during the whole experiments.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded using UV–2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was characterized by a Philips Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (Philips, Netherlands) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fluorescence measurement was carried out by using an F-4600 fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the materials was measured using an ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was collected on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer in the frequency range 4000−400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). High-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) imaging was performed on a JEOL 2100F field emission transmission electron microscope, operating at an accelerating voltage of 100 kV.

Firstly, g-C3N4 was synthesized according to the previous reported method [17]. Then, 10 mL of the obtained g-C3N4 (0.5 mg/mL) was added to a 50 mL round bottom flask. After that, 1 mL 7.9 mM H2PtCl6 was added and ultrasound for 10 min. Next, 1 mL NaBH4 (0.1 M) was slowly dropped into the solution under ultrasonic condition. After ultrasound for 180 min, the product was centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 30 min, washed two times with ultrapure water, and then dispersed in 40 mL water for further use. The synthesis process of PtNPs was the same as above without the addition of g-C3N4.

170 μL acetate buffer (10 mM, pH 4.5), 8 μL g-C3N4/PtNPs, different concentrations of Hg2+ were added into a 0.6 mL bullet tube. The mixture was incubated at room temperature for 5 min, followed by adding 200 μL TMB (1.2 mM) to the reaction mixture. The final volume of the solution was 400 μL. Then, the colorimetric reaction was allowed to proceed for 20 min. The resultant solution was scanned by a UV/Vis spectrophotometer with the wavelength range of 500–800 nm, the highest absorption peak at 650 nm was used as detection signal. The operation process of specificity experiment was the same as Hg2+, just replaced Hg2+ by other metal ions, including Ni2+, Fe3+, Co2+, K+, Mg2+, Cr2+, Na+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Pb2+ and Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the colorimetric sensor for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The Hg2+ sensing mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 1 . In the absence of Hg2+, g-C3N4/PtNPs showed strong oxidase-like activity that can catalyze the oxidation of TMB to produce colored solution. After addition of Hg2+, the catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs was depressed due to the formation of HgPt amalgam on the hybrids' surface. Thus, through recording the absorption changes of the solution, a simple colorimetric sensor can be fabricated for the rapid detection of Hg2+.

The g-C3N4/PtNPs nanocomposite was prepared by incubating g-C3N4 with H2PtCl6 and NaBH4 under ultrasonic condition. The color of the mixture changed from pale yellow to black after reaction, indicating that the Pt4+ of H2PtCl6 was reduced to Pt0 and deposited on the g-C3N4 surface. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was further used to characterize the morphology of the synthesized g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/PtNPs composites. Layer-structured g-C3N4 nanosheet can be obtained after ultrasonic exfoliation (Fig. 1 A). FTIR spectrum was performed for the investigation of chemical functional groups of g-C3N4 nanosheets (Fig. S1). The peak at 808 cm−1 is the characteristic breathing mode of triazine units [29]. The peaks in the 1200–1600 cm−1 region correspond to the typical stretching vibrations of aromatic CN heterocycles [30]. The broad band located in 3000–3500 cm−1 is related to N-H stretching modes of amino groups [31]. As shown in Fig. 1B, the PtNPs with a diameter of about 3 nm were uniformly dispersed on the surface of g-C3N4 sheets. It should be noted that the PtNPs were deposited within the g-C3N4 sheet, there is no free PtNPs found in the solution. These results indicated that the g-C3N4 sheet can be used as a good support for anchoring PtNPs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was further performed to analyze the chemical composition of the synthesized nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. S2, the element of Pt, C and N were observed in the product, which presented that the g-C3N4/PtNPs was successfully formed. From the Pt 4f electron spectra (Fig. S3), Gaussian-shaped spectrum (Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2) with a separation of about 3.3 eV was emerged. The binding energies of Pt 4f7/2 at 70.4 and 74.7 eV can be attributed to Pt0 and Pt4+, respectively [32]. Similar with the reported GQDs/AgNPs hybrid [12], the fluorescence of g-C3N4 can be quenched by the deposited PtNPs due to charge transfer processes (Fig. S4).

Fig. S5 shows the catalytic performances of g-C3N4/PtNPs for TMB in the presence of N2 (curve a), air (curve b) and H2O2 (curve c), respectively. We found that the synthesized g-C3N4/PtNPs can catalyze the oxidation of TMB both in the absence or presence of H2O2. There is no oxidation of TMB under N2 conditions. Because H2O2 is non-environmentally friendly, unstable and easy decomposition during exposure to light, and may complicate the operation procedure and decrease the system stability, we chose to use the g-C3N4/PtNPs as oxidase mimics rather than peroxidase mimics to simplify the detection procedure and improve the reproducibility.

The catalytic activities of different materials were investigated without H2O2 and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . It can be seen that the absorption of the solution was extremely low with the addition of g-C3N4 only (curve c), presenting not much oxidation of TMB occurred in the presence of g-C3N4 alone. Only a small absorption signal can be observed in the presence of PtNPs (curve b), indicating the low catalytic activity of PtNPs without the g-C3N4 as stabilizer. It is worth to note that the catalytic activity of PtNPs was greatly improved by integration with g-C3N4 (curve a), which is 4.2 times as high as that of pristine PtNPs. Thus, we concluded that g-C3N4 played a crucial role in the enhancement of catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs due to the following advantages: 1) The high number of nitrogen atoms in the networks of g-C3N4 favors the tight coordination and thus the stabilization of metal particles, which is expectedly much higher than some carbon (such as N-doped graphene, N-doped carbon nanotube etc.) supported noble-metal catalysts [33,34]. The large specific surface, rigid 2-D structure and functional group of g-C3N4, making it excellent supporter for dispersion of the PtNPs and expand the surface area of the whole nanocomposites. 2) The conjugated π structure of g-C3N4 providing it with good adsorption ability for TMB through π–π and hydrophobic interactions [35], which is beneficial to enhance the affinity toward TMB and thus improve the catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs for the oxidation of TMB. 3) g-C3N4 is often well-crystallized due to the nature of lamellar network structure, which could act as an excellent electron transport platform to facilitate the charge transfer process [36,37]. In addition, noble metal Pt is known to serve as an effective electron acceptor [37] and it possesses good conductivity, hence the deposition of PtNPs on the surfaces of g-C3N4 will further accelerate the electron transfer with TMB and O2. 4) Since PtNPs possess relatively weak oxidase-like activity, the synergistic effect resulting from the integration of g-C3N4 and PtNPs into one hybrid nanocomposite might contributes to the overall enhancements in catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs.

To further evaluate the enzyme mimic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs, the typical Michaelis–Menten curve was plotted in a certain range of TMB concentrations (Fig. S6A). The steady-state kinetic parameters were determined from a Lineweaver–Burk plot. The comparison of the kinetic parameters of different enzyme-mimic materials were shown in Table S1. The K m value for g-C3N4/PtNPs with TMB as a substrate was 0.066 mM, which is much lower than that of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), MnSe-g-C3N4, PtNPs/GO, Citrate-PtNPs, GO-Fe and HRP. These results illustrated that the affinity to TMB for g-C3N4/PtNPs is significantly higher than these materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Hg2+ on the catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Feasibility study of the g-C3N4/PtNPs based strategy for Hg2+ detection was first performed. As shown in Fig. 3 , g-C3N4-supported PtNPs hybrid could catalyze the oxidation of TMB to produce a dark blue color product with maximum absorbance at 652 nm (curve a). After introduction with 200 nM Hg2+, the catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs was to some degree inhibited, and accompanied by the color fading of TMB solution (curve b). When increasing the Hg2+ concentration to 400 nM, the absorption signal was dramatically decreased, indicating further inhibition of the catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs by Hg2+. The decrease of absorption was dose dependent, which might be resulted from the formation of HgPt amalgam on the surface of g-C3N4/PtNPs.

Fig. S6 shows steady-state kinetic assays of g-C3N4/PtNPs before and after treated with Hg2+. The apparent kinetic parameters were calculated based on the Michaelis–Menten equation: ν = V max × [S]/(K m+[S]), where ν is the initial velocity, V max is the maximal velocity, [S] is the concentration of substrate, and K
m is the Michaelis constant, which can reflect the affinity of enzyme to the substrate [38,39]. As shown in Table S2, the apparent K
m value of g-C3N4/PtNPs increased while the V max decreased after addition of Hg2+, indicating that mercury-treated g-C3N4/PtNPs has lower catalytic activity and weaker affinity to TMB substrate.

HRTEM imaging was performed to study the crystalline structure variation of g-C3N4/PtNPs before and after treated with Hg2+. Fig. S7A shows a HRTEM image taken from the g-C3N4/PtNPs nanocomposites. Typical PtNPs with well crystalline texture were observed on the surface of g-C3N4 sheets. The lattice spacing of 0.223 nm is corresponding to the (1 1 1) facet of Pt crystal [40]. It is worthwhile to note that the ultrasonication treatment during the reduction procedure does not peel off the PtNPs owing to the strong interaction between g-C3N4 and PtNPs, and the unique confined structure of PtNPs within g-C3N4 sheets, which can enhance their interfacial contact, suppress the dissolution/agglomeration of NPs. It can be found that after interaction with Hg2+, the morphology and lattice structure of PtNPs were very different from before (Fig. S7B). The measured interplanar spacing is 0.3095 nm, agreeing well with the spacing of the (0 0 2) plane in PtHg4 amalgam [41].

The interaction mechanism between Hg2+ and g-C3N4/PtNPs was further investigated by XPS measurements. As shown in Fig. S8, after interaction with Hg2+, peaks of Hg 4f and Hg 4d could be observed in the XPS spectrum of g-C3N4/PtNPs. The atomic ratio of Pt, Hg, C and N elements in g-C3N4/PtNPs/Hg was listed in Table S3 based on the XPS results. In addition, two well-characterized doublets were appeared in the Hg 4f electron spectra of g-C3N4/PtNPs after treated with Hg2+ (Fig. 4 ). The two signals of Hg 4f7/2 arose at binding energies of 99.8 and 101.3 eV were corresponding to Hg0 and Hg2+, respectively [42]. These results confirmed that a part of Hg2+ was reduced to metallic Hg0 under the experimental conditions, thereby forming the PtHg amalgam on the surface of g-C3N4/PtNPs and inhibited the catalytic activity.

Similar to natural enzyme, the catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs would be affected by the concentration of catalyst, reaction temperature and pH. In order to obtain a high sensitivity for Hg2+ detection, some experimental conditions were optimized. The absorption signal in the absence and presence of Hg2+ are denoted as A0 and A, respectively (ΔA = A0-A). As a catalyst, the amount of g-C3N4/PtNPs is an important parameter for Hg2+ sensing. The absorption signal would be increased with the increasing concentration of g-C3N4/PtNPs within a given range. However, the Hg2+ detection sensitivity would be depressed by excess g-C3N4/PtNPs. Considering above two aspects, 8 μL g-C3N4/PtNPs was used during the experiment (Fig. S9A). The pH of the reaction buffer has great effect on the TMB oxidation and the interaction between g-C3N4/PtNPs and Hg2+. Hence, the absorption responses of the catalytic system at various pH values were investigated. As shown in Fig. S9B, the highest inhibition of the catalytic activity was observed at pH of 4.5. Thus, pH 4.5 was selected for Hg2+ detection. The reaction time between Hg2+ and g-C3N4/PtNPs was also investigated. We found that the catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs was inhibited most significantly after incubation with Hg2+ for 5 min (Fig. S9C), indicating the reaction can be completed within 5 min. The catalytic time of g-C3N4/PtNPs would affect the oxidation amount of TMB thus the absorption signal. The kinetic curves of TMB oxidation catalyzed by g-C3N4/PtNPs before and after addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ were performed (Fig. S10). The absorption value reached a plateau after 20 min, so 20 min was selected as the colorimetric reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of the colorimetric assay for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the Hg2+ detection sensitivity by the colorimetric assay, absorption changes aroused by different concentrations of Hg2+ were monitored under the optimum conditions. Fig. 5 shows the absorption curves of TMB solution with the concentration of Hg2+ from 0 to 500 nM. It can be found that the absorption signal at 652 nm was proportionately decreased upon increasing concentration of Hg2+. After addition of 500 nM Hg2+ to incubate with the g-C3N4/PtNPs solution, the absorption signal was nearly completely inhibited, revealing that the sensing system is sensitive to the added Hg2+. The inset of Fig. 5 presents the absorption signal difference (ΔA) as a function of Hg2+ concentration. The absorption signal was linearly related to the Hg2+ concentration in the range of 5–500 nM. The detection limit of Hg2+ was estimated to be 1.23 nM (3σ), which is lower than the maximum allowable level (10 nM) of Hg2+ in drinking water set by the United States Environmental Protection Agency. The comparison of the presented work with other Hg2+ detection methods are shown in Table S4. The resulting sensor showed a higher or comparable sensitivity than that of those recently reported colorimetric and fluorescence assays. Such high sensitivity can be achieved without the use of unstable H2O2, expensive DNAzyme or fluorochrome, making the sensing procedure more simple and economical.

Apart from sensitivity, selectivity is also a very important indicator to evaluate the sensing performance of a system. The absorption responses of 500 nM Hg2+ and other competitive metal ions were tested under the same conditions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 , a much higher absorption changes (ΔA) was measured only in the presence of Hg2+. On the contrary, no significant decrease of absorption signal was observed by adding other metal ions into the solution of g-C3N4/PtNPs. The absorption signal was not significantly changed in the coexistence of Hg2+ with other metal ions (Fig. S11). These results suggested that the g-C3N4/PtNPs based colorimetric assay is capable of discriminating Hg2+ from other metal ions. The high selectivity for Hg2+ might be attribute to the strong binding affinity with N of g-C3N4 to fast capture Hg2+ and the specific Hg-Pt interaction to form amalgam [43].

To test its practical application ability for real sample analysis, we applied the developed colorimetric sensor to the determination of Hg2+ in Mingjiang river water samples. The original concentration of Hg2+ was detected to be 1.05 nM by ICP-MS, which is below the detection limit of the colorimetric sensor. Thus, different concentrations of Hg2+ standard solution spiked water samples were further analyzed. Each sample was tested three times. The results are listed in Table S5. The recovery of the added known amounts of Hg2+ were found to be in the range of 98.7–104.2% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) within 5%, indicating that the proposed sensor has good applicability for the analysis of Hg2+ in real sample. Operational stability is one of the major concerns for practical application of a well-established sensor. After storing for one month, the catalytic ability of g-C3N4/PtNPs to TMB oxidation was measured. Compared with the originally prepared g-C3N4/PtNPs, 97.3% of the absorption signal was retained. These results indicated that the as-prepared g-C3N4/PtNPs can be used as a robust colorimetric probe for the sensing of Hg2+ with high accuracy.

In summary, we have demonstrated a novel g-C3N4/PtNPs-based colorimetric sensing system for rapid detection of Hg2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity. Through a simple in situ reduction process, synergistic g-C3N4/PtNPs hybrid that exhibits improved oxidase-like activity was successfully obtained. This nanocomposites show several advantages over natural enzymes, such as easy preparation, low cost and high stability. The catalytic ability of g-C3N4/PtNPs can be specifically inhibited by Hg2+ through the specific Hg−Pt interaction. Taking advantage of the high catalytic activity of g-C3N4/PtNPs, as low as 1.23 nM Hg2+ can be detected by the proposed method. In addition, the nanocomposite was applicable for the analysis of Hg2+ in real water with satisfactory results. The method presented here is simple and economical, no further chemical modification or additional oxidant was needed during the sensing procedure, which greatly simplify the operation process. Therefore, this work may provide a good example for Hg2+ sensing and extend the potential application in environmental protection and clinical diagnosis by using g-C3N4-based hybrid materials as an efficient biocatalyst.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21605019), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2015J01083, 2016J01049), Fujian High-level University Project “Construction of Food Sciences and Engineering” (612014015).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2017.05.019.
